coredata.dc:title: Dual fluorophores embedded in zeolitic imidazolate framework‑8 for ratiometric fluorescence sensing of a biomarker of anthrax spores
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiaoran
Wang, Qi
Sun, Xincheng
Asif, Muhammad
Aziz, Ayesha
Zhang, Yuan
Dong, Chuan
Wang, Ruibing
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: The precise and selective monitoring of dipicolinic acid (DPA), a particular biomarker associated with anthrax spores, is crucial for the rapid detection and diagnosis of anthrax infection. A ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe called AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8, has been designed by encapsulating gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into zeolitic imidazolate framework‑8 (ZIF-8) for sensing DPA. By leveraging enrichment effects of ZIF-8, embedded AuNCs restricts intramolecular movements and triggers aggregation-induced emission, further acquiring bright fluorescence. In the presence of europium ions (Eu3+), the fluorescence of AuNCs@ZIF-8 significantly decreases due to static quenching. However, Eu3+ are sequestered by DPA through strong coordination effects, causing fluorescence recovery of AuNCs@ZIF-8. Eu3+-mediated AuNCs@ZIF-8 therefore serve as response signals for DPA recognition. Integrating with stable reference signals of GQDs, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor exhibits the satisfactory linearity of 0.1–200 µM and detection limit of 32 nM. Furthermore, the smartphone-integrated visual mode can immediately identify fluorescence colors for assessing DPA. Compared to alternative nanoprobes, this ratiometric fluorescence sensor demonstrates higher sensitivity, lowering than DPA commonly released by infectious spores (60 µM). This study not only provides the promising sensor for DPA measurement during early anthrax diagnosis, but offers insights on designing ratiometric sensors with diverse fluorescence nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Zeolite-like imidazole frameworks
Aggregation induced emission
Anthrax biomarker
Visual sensing

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The mechanisms of fluorescence sensing of DPA
Selectivity of the nanoprobe toward DPA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274090) and Shanxi Province Graduate Student Education Innovation Project (2023KY022).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of materials. TEM images. a) AuNCs, b) GQDs, c) ZIF-8, d) Eu3+- AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe. SEM images of e) ZIF-8. f) Eu3+- AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe. g) EDX spectrum, h-o) elemental mapping of Eu3+- AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe. p) Nitrogen sorption isotherms of ZIF-8 and Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs-ZIF-8 nanoprobe. q) FTIR spectra of AuNCs, GQDs, ZIF-8, and Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs-ZIF-8 nanoprobe. r) The XPS of Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs-ZIF-8 nanoprobe.

Optical properities. a) UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNCs, GQDs, Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe. b) Fluorescence spectra of AuNCs, GQDs, ZIF-8, nanoprobe (Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8) and nanoprobe + 120 μM DPA. c) Fluorescence spectra of AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 after adding different Eu3+ concentration. d) Effect of Eu3+ concentration on fluorescence for AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8. e) Effect of different pH values on fluorescence of the Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 before and after adding DPA. f) Response time of Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe to DPA.

DPA detection. a) Fluorescence spectra of Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe with adding increased concentrations of DPA. b) Calibration plot of DPA detection using Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe. c) Photographs of Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe based strips for visual sensing DPA under 365 nm UV lamps and fitting curve of fluorescence chroma between (R /(G + B)) and DPA employing smartphone APP.
Selectivity of the Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe to DPA (200 μM) and related interfering substances (1 mM).

Real-time DPA determination. Fluorescence graphs of the cultured cells and various concentrations of B. subtilis spore suspension (from 1 to 3: 5.48 × 107, 2.74 × 108, 5.48 × 108 spore/mL) with the Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe. Hela cells incubated with the Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe before (a–d) and after adding increased DPA (e-h, i-l).m) Fluorescence images after incubating various concentrations of B. subtilis spore suspension. n) The bar graph showed the variations in the fluorescence ratio from red and blue channels (Ired/Iblue) acquired from ImageJ. o) strips and smartphone integrated Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe for visual detecting B. subtilis spores from 1 to 3. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Flow chart diagram illustrating the synthesis of Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs-ZIF-8 nanoprobe and their application for DPA sensing.

head.ce:title: Dual fluorophores embedded in zeolitic imidazolate framework‑8 for ratiometric fluorescence sensing of a biomarker of anthrax spores

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Department, Taiyuan Institute of Technology,Taiyuan 030008, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Department, Taiyuan Institute of Technology,Taiyuan 030008, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Quality Research in Chinese Medicine, Institute of Chinese Medical Sciences, University of Macau, Taipa, Macau 999078, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Quality Research in Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Quality Research in Chinese Medicine, Institute of Chinese Medical Sciences, University of Macau, Taipa, Macau 999078, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The precise and selective monitoring of dipicolinic acid (DPA), a particular biomarker associated with anthrax spores, is crucial for the rapid detection and diagnosis of anthrax infection. A ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe called AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8, has been designed by encapsulating gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into zeolitic imidazolate framework‑8 (ZIF-8) for sensing DPA. By leveraging enrichment effects of ZIF-8, embedded AuNCs restricts intramolecular movements and triggers aggregation-induced emission, further acquiring bright fluorescence. In the presence of europium ions (Eu3+), the fluorescence of AuNCs@ZIF-8 significantly decreases due to static quenching. However, Eu3+ are sequestered by DPA through strong coordination effects, causing fluorescence recovery of AuNCs@ZIF-8. Eu3+-mediated AuNCs@ZIF-8 therefore serve as response signals for DPA recognition. Integrating with stable reference signals of GQDs, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor exhibits the satisfactory linearity of 0.1–200 µM and detection limit of 32 nM. Furthermore, the smartphone-integrated visual mode can immediately identify fluorescence colors for assessing DPA. Compared to alternative nanoprobes, this ratiometric fluorescence sensor demonstrates higher sensitivity, lowering than DPA commonly released by infectious spores (60 µM). This study not only provides the promising sensor for DPA measurement during early anthrax diagnosis, but offers insights on designing ratiometric sensors with diverse fluorescence nanomaterials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Anthrax is an extremely dangerous and deadly infectious disease caused by exposure to anthrax spores [1,2]. It can have severe impacts on human health through various means, including contact with infected animals through the skin, respiratory system, and digestive tract [3,4]. Exposure to a high concentration of anthrax spores, specifically inhalation of more than 104 spores, can lead to severe health risks. Without prompt and appropriate treatment, individuals exposed to such a high dose of spores may experience life-threatening consequences within a timeframe of approximately 36 h [5]. Additionally, anthrax spores exhibit a remarkable resistance to harsh environmental conditions, allowing them to remain viable and capable of proliferation even after the bacteria themselves have been destroyed. This characteristic increases the risk of infection among humans, as the spores can persist in the environment for extended periods [6]. Therefore, it is imperative for expanding effective strategies for the early diagnosis of anthrax.

Dipicolinic acid (DPA) is a crucial and unique component of bacterial spores, constituting approximately 5 %-15 % of the dry weight of the spores. This compound serves as a distinguishing feature of anthrax spores, as it is not present in other common bacterial spores [7]. Therefore, sensitive monitoring of DPA can play a vital role in the accurate identification and diagnosis of anthrax infections [8,9]. However, rapid and accurate detection of DPA remains elusive.

Currently, the primary clinical strategies for detecting anthrax involve evaluating gene sequences and utilizing polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) technologies. PCR amplifies specific DNA sequences associated with DPA production, while ELISA detects and quantifies DPA levels using specific antibodies. Unfortunately,the extended testing process for anthrax spores not only delays the rapid detection of the disease but also raises the risk of infection [10,11]. As a result, early diagnosis primarily emphasizes precise tracking and safe evaluation to prevent any disruptive infections. In recent years, fluorescence sensing methods have garnered significant attention from researchers for detecting DPA because of their exceptional advantages, covering high spatial and temporal resolution, rapid response, high sensitivity, and direct visualization [5,7,12].Currently, most fluorescence detection approaches provide single-fluorescence output signals, which may not effectively overcome interferences from background fluorescence, instrument fluctuation, and variations in operational conditions. Therefore, development of ratiometric fluorescence methods that allow for self-calibration and enhanced sensing precision is highly desirable [13–15]. In this regard, the pioneering work by Huskens's group in 2010 laid the foundation for ratiometric fluorescence sensing in the detection of DPA. They established blue naphthalene emission as the reference signal and red Eu3+ emission as the response signal, utilizing the antenna effect to monitor DPA [16]. Since then, plethora of nanomaterials as internal signals have been applied for combining Eu3+ to develop ratiometric fluorescent sensors, such as SiNPs, QDs, nanosheets and so on [17–19]. Unfortunately, in terms of practical application, short excitation wavelength (250–300 nm) and the excessive consumption of Eu3+ during synthesis pose challenges in terms of resource utilization and non-destructive detection of bacteria [16,20]. To address this problem, Yilmaz et al. [21] introduced fixed amounts ions-mediated probe based on competitive coordination effect to monitor DPA. The excitation wavelength depends on as-prepared probes, and ion contents can be fixed by fluorescence changes, which is efficient to avoid excessive damage and resource depletion. However, few ratiometric fluorescence probes are capable of accomplishing this. Consequently, the development of new probe materials is highly desirable for achieving the ratiometric fluorescence assay.

Metal organic framework (MOF), a rising probe material, has demonstrated noteworthy advancements for sensing and monitoring by virtue of tailored porous, large specific surface area, adjustable metal ions species and diverse functional sites [22–24]. Particularly as a result of its simple preparation, exceptional biodegradability, and capability to be functionalized with various fluorophores, zeolite-like imidazole framework (ZIF-8) has emerged as a highly promising contender among metal organic frameworks (MOFs) for sensitive monitoring of biomarkers [25–27]. Since first breakthrough reported that Chen et al. [28] encapsulated BPEI-CQDs to ZIF-8 toward realizing Cu2+ sensing, an obvious increase in the research on the fluorescence of ZIF-8 had been witnessed. Compared with other traditional sensors, ZIF-8 based fluorescence sensors not only immobilize and protect fluorophores within them, but also offer greater porosity to enrich analytes in order to optimize sensitivity [29]. Although the majority of sensors experience quenched fluorescence and significantly limit sensing capabilities after embedding common fluorophores, this is primarily due to the fact that fluorophores aggregate and generate possible π–π interactions during the enrichment process of ZIF-8. This further depletes excited state energy and induces quenching fluorescence [30]. Therefore, the potential for designing and modulating ZIF-8 materials with high fluorescence is substantial. In 2018, Wang et al firstly encapsulated AuNCs onto ZIF-8 to enhance10-fold fluorescence by enriching and restricting free movements of AuNCs, further realizing aggregation-induced fluorescence enhancement (AIE) effect [31]. Currently, several AIE-typed AuNCs@ZIF-8 ratiometric probes have been fabricated in recent years that followed with the goal of enhancing response signal amplification and serving as an ideal tool for increasing sensitivity [32,33]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), with superior good photostability and low toxicity, are considered as attractive reference materials for constructing ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe [34–36]. Inspired by this, an improved approach to sensing biomarkers with high sensitivity can involve the utilization of GQDs possessing dual-emission properties in conjunction with functional AIE-typed AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanoprobes.

In this study, we present the development of a ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe by incorporating gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) onto a zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) matrix for sensing DPA. The AuNCs exhibit orange fluorescence with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) properties, which can be quenched by Eu3+ through coordination effects. However, the presence of DPA restores the fluorescence of AuNCs. On the other hand, GQDs exhibit stable blue fluorescence to Eu3+ and DPA compared to AuNCs. By harnessing the protective and adsorption capabilities of ZIF-8, the bright fluorescence response of AuNCs, and the stable signals of GQDs, our nanoprobe possesses the ability to avoid interference from the surrounding environment and enhance the accuracy of DPA detection. Additionally, the fluorescence color variations of the strips loaded with the nanoprobe can be easily recognized and converted to RGB values using smartphones under UV lights, enabling visual evaluation of DPA on the spot. This ratiometric fluorescence detection method, which utilizes variations in fluorescence intensity and color, serves as a benchmark for subsequent monitoring of hazardous substances.

In this work, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor was constructed with the introduction of two fluorescence materials including AuNCs and GQDs onto the ZIF-8 by a simple one pot method to realize sensitive and specificdetection of DPA. The schematic synthesis procedure has been displayed in Scheme 1 a. AuNCs interacted with europium ions formed fluorescence response signals, while GQDs almost kept unchanged and regarded as fluorescence reference signals. When the mix of DPA and Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8, the increase orange fluorescence of AuNCs may be observed because of the greater interaction forces between europium ion and DPA than AuNCs. Scheme 1b illustrated the detailed process for detecting DPA. A ratiometric and visual dual-color fluorescence detection method was therefore constructed to rapidly monitor DPA concentrations.

Some characterizations were carried out to make sure the successful synthesis of the Eu3+- AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8. TEM images showed the morphology and micro-structure of AuNCs, GQDs, ZIF-8 and Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8. The TEM analysis in Fig. 1 a and b revealed that the mean sizes of AuNCs and GQDs were approximately 2 nm. Fig. 1c and d demonstrated that Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe shared the identical rhombic dodecahedron configuration with pure ZIF-8, showing average diameter of 106 nm. Moreover, some clear dots could be noticed in the TEM of Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8, indicating the encapsulation of AuNCs and GQDs onto the ZIF-8. The EDS elemental mappings indicated that Au, Zn, C, S, Eu, N, and O elements were clearly found throughout the nanoprobe in Fig. S1, thereby authenticating the efficient synthesis of Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe. In the Fig. 1e and f, SEM spectra further indicated as-constructed Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe with embedding AuNCs and GQDs did not change the shape of ZIF-8, still remaining a rhombohedral dodecahedron with sizes of 106 nm. Well-distinguished peaks (Fig. 1g) for C, N, O, Au, S, Zn and Eu elements were observed on their own corresponding positions in the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) spectrum. C, N, O, Au, S, Zn and Eu elements in EDX elemental mapping were homogeneously distributed throughout the nanoprobe from Fig. 1h to Fig. 1o. According to DLS data (Fig. S2), the average particle sizes of ZIF-8 and Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe were about 112 nm, which was consistent with TEM and SEM results. These indicated that the encapsulation of AuNCs and GQDs would not change the corresponding size and structure of ZIF-8.

To further testify that the embedding of AuNCs and GQDs did not vary the shape of ZIF-8, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out. The crystallinity and phase purity were characterized through XRD analysis as shown in Fig. S3. Comparing the ZIF-8 and Eu3+-AuNCs /GQDs@ZIF-8, the identical diffraction peak locations were detected: 2θ = 7.3°, 10.4◦, and 12.7◦, as indicated by ZIF-8′s (011), (002), and (112), respectively. The phenomenon not only demonstrated the high phase purity, but also was the strong evidence that the decoration of AuNCs and GQDs did not affect the integrity of crystal. It may have been impossible to discern the characteristic diffraction peaks of AuNCs and GQDs due to the fact that significantly reduced peak intensities were exhibited by the ultra-small particles of AuNCs and GQDs in comparison to ZIF-8 crystal diffraction. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of the synthesized ZIF-8 and Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 were also explored. As shown in Fig. S4, the staged oxidation removal process in ZIF-8 also appeared in the TGA curve of Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8, which verified ZIF-8 maintained the integrity of frame after encapsulating fluorescent nanomaterials. Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe had slightly more weight loss than ZIF-8, indicating the partial weight loss contributed by Eu3+-AuNCs and GQDs.

Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis, were also carried out to verify successful preparation of Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs-ZIF-8 nanoprobe. As portrayed in Fig. 1p, BET data revealed that the surface areas of Eu3+-AuNCs /GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe and pure ZIF-8 were 867 m2/g and 1494 m2/g respectively, further verifying adding AuNCs and GQDs caused the reduced adsorption of ZIF-8 nanoparticles. The data were also able to make clear that the nanoprobe was microporous because of a quick adsorption increase at a low pressure (P/P0). The average size of AuNCs and GQDs were both about 2 nm, which were larger than the pores of ZIF-8 (1.16 nm) in Fig. S5, so they were encapsulated onto the skeleton of ZIF-8 and blocked the pores of ZIF-8, making a decrease of the surface area.

In Fig. 1q, the chemical bonds of AuNCs, GQDs, ZIF-8, and Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe were displayed by FTIR spectra. The peak situation in the range of 3283 cm−1 was in relation to the stretching vibrations of the OH and NH bands, while the peaks appeared at 752 and 1402 cm−1 were the ZnO and CN stretching vibrations, indicating that Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe exhibited similar absorption peaks with pure ZIF-8. Obviously, most similar characteristic peaks corresponding to AuNCs and GQDs were found in Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe, confirming that AuNCs and GQDs were well adsorbed on ZIF-8 nanocrystals.

Moreover, X rays photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was demonstrated to investigate elemental components of Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe as displayed in Fig. 1r and S6. The wide-scan XPS spectrum showed the composition of Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe covered elements of C, N, O, Zn, Eu, S, and Au. The partial spectrum of Au 4f (Fig. S6e) was separated into two peaks at 84.0 eV and 87.7 eV, which were corresponding to Au 4f5/2 and Au 4f7/2 respectively. The N 1s spectrum exhibited two peaks at 399.1 and 400.2 eV corresponding to C-N, N-H. In the Eu 3d partial spectrum (Fig. S6g), two component peaks were obtained at 1133.1 and 1160.2 eV, which related to Eu 3d5/2 and Eu 3d3/2, respectively. Fig. S6 also demonstrated characterized peaks of C 1s, O 1s, Zn 2p and S 2p spectra, further verifying successful preparation of Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe. The zeta potentials of AuNCs (−9.34 mV) and GQDs (−14.30 mV) indicated negative charges, whereas ZIF-8 possessed a zeta potential of + 27.88 mV as shown in Fig. S7. The final nanoprobe synthesized demonstrated a positive charge (+16.42 mV) and occupied a potential between Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs and ZIF-8. This finding confirmed the existence of an electrostatic interaction between the nanoprobe. As a whole, the Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite was successfully constructed based on the above characterization.

The optical properties of Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 have been shown as follows including UV–visible spectrum (UV–vis) and fluorescence spectrum (FL). In Fig. 2 a, the UV–vis spectra of Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe owned board absorption band from 200 nm to 600 nm.

To study the emission properties of AuNCs, GQDs, AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 and Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe, the FL spectra were studied in Fig. 2b. When excited at 365 nm, two fluorescence emission bands appeared at 445 nm and 580 nm can be obviously noticed in AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8, respectively corresponding to the emission locations of AuNCs and GQDs rather than pure ZIF-8, clearly confirming that the AuNCs and GQDs were embedded onto the ZIF-8. Moreover, In Fig. S8, the intensity of AuNCs@ZIF-8 was approximately 4.84 times higher than that of AuNCs, and the emitted fluorescence wavelength has the small blue shift about 18 nm. Fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield (QY) of AuNCs in AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 was also measured by 11.46 μs and 5.38 % and were higher than those of AuNCs (fluorescence lifetime and QY of 6.34 μs and 0.95 %, respectively) in Table S1, further indicating that the successful synthesis of AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 endowed AIE properties and significantly improved the emission performance of AuNCs.

To study the fluorescence sensing capacities of DPA, following experiments were carried out as shown in Fig. S9, with the introduction of Eu3+, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 at 580 nm was largely quenched, while the fluorescence intensity at 445 nm remained invariable relative to GQDs, proving that the fluorescence of AuNCs can be quenched by Eu3+ without affecting the luminescence of GQDs. After introducing DPA to Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8, it was found that the emission intensity at 580 nm recovered significantly and the emission intensity at 445 nm remained constant, which was consistent with the fluorescence detection results of single GQDs and AuNCs, therefore it was considered as a ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe for determining DPA. The long-term stability of Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe before and after adding DPA (120 µM) were investigated in Fig. S10. After 2 h continuous irradiation of 365 nm UV lamps in Fig. S10a, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I580/I445) of the fluorescence sensor changed slightly, indicating designed nanoprobe exhibited extremely strong resistance to photobleaching before and after adding DPA. As shown in Fig. S10b, the Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe for sensing DPA was in the different storage temperatures, and the results demonstrated there was almost no distinct fluorescence variations. In Fig. S10c, the slight change in fluorescence ratio (I580/I445) at room temperature for 14 days indicated that this sensing analysis for DPA owned extremely strong long-term stability. Interestingly, added 120 μM DPA to the nanoprobe before and after storage at 5 °C for 30 days, the fluorescence of nanoprobe could still keep the similar enhanced trends, testifying that the ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe remained stable after being placed for 30 days and possessed strong fluorescence sensing ability for DPA in Fig. S10d. Above results verified Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe had excellent long-term fluorescence stabilities and sensing abilities under various storage conditions of irriadition time, temperatures and placed days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanisms of fluorescence sensing of DPA
ce:section.ce:para: The possible quenching mechanism of the AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 mediated by Eu3+ was validated. The quenched fluorescence at 580 nm of AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 can be ascribed to dynamic quenching, static quenching, or both of them. In the dynamic quenching process, the quenching constant will increase with the increase of temperature as well as the occurrence of changing fluorescence lifetimes because of the collision between fluoroprobe and quenchers, causing the deactivation of excited states of fluoroprobe. Conversely, the quenching constant will descend with the rise of temperature for the static quenching process and the fluorescence lifetimes have barely variations due to the formation of fluoroprobe and quenchers [37]. Therefore, the quenching type of the AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 could be obtained by testing the quenching constant through Stern-Volmer equation. In Fig. S11, the quenching constant showed higher values at lower temperatures, indicating that the process of quenching orange fluorescence would be static quenching. In addition, the fluorescence lifetimes of AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8, Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe and DPA-Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe were investigated as presented in Table S1, and their corresponding values of fluorescence lifetimes were 11.46 µs, 11.41 µs and 11.44 µs respectively, thereby identifying that the fluorescence lifetimes remained basically unchanged before and after the reaction, which revealed the static quenching reaction process between Eu3+ and AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8. From the above experiment, the fluorescence of AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 for Eu3+ might be caused due to static quenching by energy transmission. Eu3+ can coordinate with the oxygen-donor atoms of the carboxylate groups on the surface of AuNCs@ZIF-8, thus resulting in building the bridge for effective energy transfer from AuNCs@ZIF-8 to the Eu3+. This decreased the electron cloud density of AuNCs@ZIF-8 and quenched their fluorescence. The effects of other rare-earth ions contanining La3+, Ce4+, Pr3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Gd3+, Tb3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+, Yb3+, Lu3+, Sc3+ on the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 were investigated. Fig. S12 verified adding other rare-earth ions caused slight changes at 580 nm and 445 nm, respectively. This testified Eu3+ could act as the superiority modulator for tailoring the fluorescence ratio (I580/I445) of AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 for following experiment.

According to previous report, DPA contained two carboxyl groups and had strong affinity for lanthanide elements, which can make the strong coordination between them and remove Eu3+ from the nanoprobe surfaces. The fluorescence recovery mechanism could be verified by following experiments. After introducing 120 μM DPA, the orange fluorescence at 580 nm of nanoprobe started to recover and the colors changed from blue to pink red under UV lamps in Fig. S13a. This was due to that DPA owned the tridentate ligand and had a greater binding force with Eu3+ than AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8. As illustrated in Fig. S13b, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 was barely affected by DPA addition, further proving that the fluorescence restoration of nanoprobe was due to the removal of Eu3+ from AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8. Fig. S14a further showed that after adding DPA caused the disappearing characteristic peak of Eu3+ in the XPS spectrum, and the Zeta potential in Fig. S14b decreased from + 16.40 to + 10.27 mV after adding DPA to Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe, further elucidating the stronger coordination between Eu3+ and DPA than AuNCs. In addition, Fig. S15 displayed the significant decrease of the elemental mappings of Eu3+ after the effect of DPA and nanoprobe, and the EDX spectrum in Fig. S16 also demonstrated the nanoprobe contained almost no Eu3+ content, which testified that DPA could interact with Eu3+ and remove Eu3+ from the surface of nanoprobe.

To verify the coordination interaction between Eu3+ and DPA, other chelators including ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA), bipyridine (BIPY), terpyridine (TPY), nitrilotriacetic acid (NTA), iminodiacetic acid (IDA), diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA) and ethylenediamine-N,N'-diacetic acid (EDDA) were studied in competition with Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8. As potratyed in Fig. S17, the fluorescence of AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 basically kept unchanged, while the quenched fluorescence of Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 gradually recovered, just like the addition of DPA. This indicated that Eu3+ could form the stable conjugate with strong chelators, further verifying the competitive coordination interaction between Eu3+ and DPA. However, due to the rarity of the above-mentioned coordination agents in anthrax spores, they had extremely low interference for DPA detection in actual sample.

The coordination relationship between the DPA and Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 was explored by the Job’s plot, mass spectra and nuclear magnetic resonance. As shown in Fig. S18, the Job’s plot proved that the fluorescence intensity reached to the maxium when the stoichiometric ratio between DPA and Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 was 1:2. Additionallity, This also can be demonstrated by High-Resolution Mass Spectrometry. In Fig. S19, only matrix peaks appeared in the mass spectrometry of the Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8. After adding DPA, a obvious peak at m/z = 482.93436 (calc: 4482.9336) appeared in Fig. S20, corresponding to [2DPA-Eu]+. In addition, the coordination interaction between DPA and Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe was also analyzed by 1H NMR. As shown in Fig. S21, 1H NMR spectrum of AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe after adding Eu3+ exhibited significant quenching effects of peaks due to the strong paramagnetic character of Eu3+ (Xe 4f7 6 s2) [38]. The subsequent addition of DPA caused the intermittence of the NMR peaks, which was originated that the coordination interaction between DPA and Eu3+ reduced the paramagnetic characteristics of the Eu3+. This also verified that DPA can strongly coordinate with Eu3+ in Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe.

To ensure the sensing process in the best conditions, related parameters were investigated to ensure optimal experimental progress. As portrayed in Fig. 2c and 2d, one of the parameters was europium ions concentration, the orange fluorescence at 580 nm became weak gradually by adding increased Eu3+ concentrations, when the Eu3+ concentrations were added up to 60 μM, the fluorescence intensity no longer decreased, so the selected Eu3+ concentration was 60 μM. Another important indicator was pH, the various buffer types and pH values were researched for optimizing the best conditions. Before and after introducing DPA, the fluorescence intensity ratio of nanoprobe in common buffer types were studied under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. S22, The Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe in PBS and BR buffer solutions for fluorescence sensing of DPA was unreliable, as the phosphate groups they contained could interact with Zn2+ in the ZIF-8 matrix, further leading to structural damage of the nanoprobe. And there was no significant difference in HEPES, NaAc-HAc, and Tris-HCl buffers for the nanoprobe, while Tris-HCl exhibited better fluorescence recovery ability after adding DPA. Therefore, Tris-HCl buffers were selected as the optimal buffer system for following experiments. As pH increased from 3 to 12 in Fig. 2e, Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 was almost stable under neutral and alkaline conditions due to the degradation of ZIF-8 under acidic conditions. Therefore, it was also super to choose pH 7.4 to satisfy physiological condition during detecting process. As an important parameter, reaction time was recorded in Fig. 2f, after mixing DPA with Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8, the I580/I445 reached to maximum in 180 s, so the best reaction time was 180 s.

To further testify the linear range between Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 and DPA, varied concentrations of DPA were introduced to nanoprobe and emission spectra were recorded under the excitation of 365 nm in Fig. 3 . Increased DPA concentrations resulted in significantly enhanced fluorescence of AuNCs at 580 nm and the fluorescence of GQDs at 445 nm kept unchanged. It clearly showed DPA concertation was correlated with the ratio of fluorescence intensity (580 nm/445 nm). I580/I445 was proportional to the concentration of DPA and linearly increased in the range of 0.1 to 200 μM (R2 = 0.9968), demonstrating an ultralow detection limit of 32 nM (3σ/k). Notably, the fluorescence response of Eu3+-AuNCs also changed with the introduction of DPA within narrow lineaity of 10–40 μM in Fig. S23, which corresponded to the LOD of 3.26 μM. Compared to the original Eu3+-AuNCs, the Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe exhibited the superior sensitivity for DPA response, which was mainly ascribed to the porous structure and large surface of ZIF-8, further facilitating the adsorption and enrichment of DPA and enhancing the contact area between the nanoprobe and DPA molecules.

To achieve the visual and quantitative recognitions for the biomarker of anthrax spores, Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 was printed on the inexpensive and feasible test strips. As the increased concentrations of DPA were dropped to strips, the color of fluorescence strips changed after 180 s and could be caught via naked eyes under UV lamps. As depicted in Fig. 3c, varied DPA concentrations were evidently capable for producing a range of colors, establishing a solid groundwork for subsequent fluorescence visualization and quantitative monitoring for DPA on site.

The smartphone-integrated color recognizer was able to extract the colors of pictures and determine the optical primary colors values (R, G, B) of each photo, ensuring the quantitative and visual analysis of anthrax spore biomarker. After adding DPA, the colors of the strips embedded with ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe changed from blue to purple and finally pink-red, and their colors were easily captured by smartphone APP to output RGB values, realizing the on-site fluorescence colorimetric sensing. The ratio of R/(G + B) and concentrations of DPA (0.25–100 μM) exhibited an excellent linear relationship with y = 0.00501x + 0.06804 and an ultralow detection limit of 80.06 nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The device offered an exceptional solution to address the drawback of transporting cumbersome instruments and enable comprehensive visual and point-of-care monitoring on the spot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the nanoprobe toward DPA
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity and selectivity of Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe for detecting DPA were also evaluated. DPA and some other possible interfering analytes were incubated with Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 , introducing 200 μM DPA remarkably enhanced the fluorescence intensity during reaction system and changed the I580/I445, while a negligible changes of nanoprobe were observed in the appearance of 1 mM interferences, further validating the nanoprobe owned good specificity and selectivity to be considered for sensing DPA in cells and bacterial spores.

The cellular biotoxicity of the Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe was investigated (Fig. S24). We used MTT assays to incubate HeLa cells with different concentrations of probe. When the probe concentration of 50 µg/mL, the cell viabilities remained more than 80 %. Then Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe was employed for imaging exogenous DPA in Fig. 5 a–l. When only nanoprobe was added to the HeLa cells, the cells showed blue fluorescence and weak red fluorescence. When increased concentrations of DPA were added to the cells, the fluorescence in red channels was significantly enhanced, while blue fluorescence kept almost unchanged. Therefore, the probe could specifically detect DPA.

The Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe can significantly monitor DPA with high selectivity and sensitivity. It is therefore effective for directly recognizing B. anthrax spores. Unfortunately, the fatality and infectivity of B. anthrax spores make it unsuitable to use B. anthrax spores as detecting objects. The non-infectious B. subtilis spores were safe and can replace B. anthrax spores for conducting fluorescence microscopic imaging experiments. As shown in Fig. 5m and n, it should be noted that when the Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe were interacted with varied B. subtilis spores (5.48 × 107, 2.74 × 108, 5.48 × 108 spores/mL), the fluorescence signal in red channels enhanced with the increasing B. subtilis spores, while intensity of blue channels maintained unchanged. In addition, ratiometric fluorescence signals (Ired/Iblue) were obtained in Fig. 5m. Moreover, the apparent fluorescence color variations could be observed in Fig. 5o, indicating fluorescence strips method can efficiently sense DPA of B. subtilis spores. The aforementioned outstanding evidence indicated Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe was available for detecting B. subtilis spores.

The designed Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe could accurately evaluate DPA with the wide range of 0.1–200 μM and a low detection limit (LOD) of 32 nM in actual samples, satisfying the efficiently detecting spores that have reached infection concentrations (60 μM). Especially, to further determine the sensitivity and practicability of the nanoprobe for sensing DPA, the LOD value and linearity range were compared with other reliable sensors. As shown in Table S2, the LOD value for DPA by Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe was significantly lower than that of most fluorescence sensors in the similar wide range, indicating the nanoprobe possessed high sensitivity. However, compared to the low LOD of Tb-PTA-OH, 2D Eu-MOGs, and Pal@FL@UiO-66-(COOH)2-Eu sensors, the nanoprobe exhibited wider detection range. This results verified that the Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 simultaneously met the requirements of a wider linear range and lower LOD values compared to other sensors, exhibiting the high sensitivity and practicability for detecting anthrax spores in actual samples. Aditionallity, In comparison with some semi-quantitative analysis of fluorescence colors that only underwent slight changes during the sensing process, integrating smartphone and fluorescence colors sensor system with large-scale variations, was more significant for achieving the real-time and on-site determination of DPA in complex anthrax spores samples.

In summary, we have developed a novel dual-emissive ratiometric nanoprobe by incorporating AuNCs and GQDs onto the inner and outer surfaces of ZIF-8. This nanoprobe enables dual-mode sensing of DPA through fluorescence intensity and color variations facilitated by Eu3+. The quenching of orange fluorescence in AuNCs mediated by Eu3+ serves as the response signal to DPA, while the unchanged blue fluorescence of GQDs acts as the reference signal. Upon exposure to DPA, the quenched orange fluorescence of AuNCs is restored due to the stronger coordination between Eu3+ and DPA compared to AuNCs. This ratiometric fluorescence strategy exhibits excellent sensitivity, with a linear range of 0.1–200 µM and a detection limit of 32 nM. Delightfully, Eu3+-AuNCs/GQDs@ZIF-8 nanoprobe loaded strips could be facilely integrated into a portable and smart color variation indicating device and enables real-time DPA monitoring. Compared to other documented sensors, the dual-mode nanoprobe demonstrates more reliable outcomes in detecting the anthrax biomarker DPA. It offers a dynamic range that spans three orders of magnitude, covering quantities smaller than the infectious dose of spores (60 μM) released by anthrax. Moreover, this nanoprobe presents a novel approach for subsequent health diagnosis and environmental monitoring.

Xiaoran Zhang: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Qi Wang: Writing – review & editing. Xincheng Sun: Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Muhammad Asif: Writing – review & editing. Ayesha Aziz: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Yuan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Chuan Dong: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Ruibing Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Shaomin Shuang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274090) and Shanxi Province Graduate Student Education Innovation Project (2023KY022).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.151582.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
